13.1.title: Six द्वितीया objects of वेदितुम् इच्छामि
13.1.body: Arjuna stacks six accusatives — प्रकृतिम्, पुरुषम्, क्षेत्रम्, क्षेत्रज्ञम्, ज्ञानम्, ज्ञेयम् — all governed by the infinitive वेदितुम् under इच्छामि (लट्, उत्तम, एकवचन). The च and एव punctuate the three pairs, signalling that this is not one question but a programmatic agenda for the entire chapter.

13.1.title: The interpolated verse that sets the agenda
13.1.body: This verse is absent from Śaṅkara's recension but supplies the six technical terms Krishna will unpack in the remaining 34 verses. केशव in सम्बोधन identifies the addressee and frames the request as a pupil's formal प्रश्न, not a battlefield exchange.

13.2.title: The इति-abhidhīyate naming pattern
13.2.body: इदं शरीरम् … क्षेत्रम् इति अभिधीयते uses the classic इति-quotative with the passive अभिधीयते (लट् कर्मणि) to *define* the body as field. The कौन्तेय vocative is metrical filler but also marks this as the chapter's foundational technical move — naming the two terms before unpacking them.

13.2.title: क्षेत्रज्ञ — the one who knows the field
13.2.body: एतद् यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञः इति — a relative-correlative (यः … तम्) wraps a second इति-naming, with प्राहुः (परोक्ष, प्रथम, बहुवचन) reporting the consensus of तद्विदः. Two definitions in one verse: kṣetra = body, kṣetrajña = body's knower.

13.3.title: क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि — the identification
13.3.body: विद्धि (लोट्, मध्यम, एकवचन from √विद्) takes a double accusative: माम् and क्षेत्रज्ञम्. Krishna collapses the duality his own question raised — the knower in *every* field (सर्वक्षेत्रेषु, सप्तमी बहुवचन) is identified with the speaker himself.

13.3.title: मतं मम — the authorial seal
13.3.body: The closing मतं मम (नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा with implied अस्ति) marks Krishna's signed opinion on what genuine ज्ञानम् is: the knowledge of *both* kṣetra and kṣetrajña — षष्ठी द्विवचन क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोः pins it to that pair, not to one or the other in isolation.

13.4.title: Five यद् interrogatives cascading
13.4.body: तत् क्षेत्रं यत्, यादृक्, यद्विकारि, यतः, यत् — five यद्-forms (relative pronoun, indefinite-like usage) frame five questions about the field, with a sixth pair (यः, यत्प्रभावः) for the knower. शृणु (लोट्, मध्यम, एकवचन) caps the verse with the imperative *listen briefly* (समासेन).

13.4.title: मे शृणु — instrumental-style genitive
13.4.body: मे here functions as the agent-genitive ('from me') with शृणु, a common Vedic-flavoured construction that English would render with 'from'. Krishna announces his exposition method: condensed (समासेन, तृतीया एकवचन) — a stylistic flag for the dense verses to follow.

13.5.title: Three तृतीया agents legitimising the teaching
13.5.body: ऋषिभिः, छन्दोभिः, ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैः — three instrumentals in बहुवचन naming the prior sources: seers, Vedic metres, and the aphorisms of the Brahma-sūtras. गीतम् (कर्मणि कृदन्त, नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा) agrees with the implied subject 'this teaching'.

13.5.title: हेतुमद्भिः विनिश्चितैः — the rationality marker
13.5.body: The qualifiers हेतुमद्भिः (with-reasons) and विनिश्चितैः (well-determined) in तृतीया बहुवचन insist that the upcoming exposition is not mere assertion but logically argued. पृथक् (अव्यय) emphasises the *distinct* approaches the prior tradition took to the same subject.

13.6.title: The Sāṅkhya enumeration in नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्लुरल
13.6.body: महाभूतानि, इन्द्रियाणि — neuter plurals listing the field's gross contents. The compound number दश एकम् च (10+1 = the 11 indriyas including manas) and पञ्च … इन्द्रियगोचराः (the 5 sense-objects) reproduces the Sāṅkhya 24-tattva inventory inside Krishna's क्षेत्र.

13.6.title: अव्यक्तम् — the unmanifest as field-content
13.6.body: By naming अव्यक्तम् alongside बुद्धिः and अहङ्कारः, the verse folds primordial prakṛti itself into the kṣetra. The field is not just the body — it is the entire psycho-cosmic apparatus that the kṣetrajña witnesses.

13.7.title: Seven subjective modes completing the field
13.7.body: इच्छा, द्वेषः, सुखम्, दुःखम्, सङ्घातः, चेतना, धृतिः — a mixed-gender list of seven mental/affective modes added to verse 6's objective tattvas. The verb उदाहृतम् (कर्मणि कृदन्त, नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन) agrees with क्षेत्रम्, sealing the inventory.

13.7.title: सविकारम् — with-its-modifications
13.7.body: The सह-prefix compound सविकारम् (अव्ययीभाव-style adverbial) says the field has just been described *together with* its mutations. समासेन (तृतीया as adverb) repeats the methodology flag from 13.4 — this was the condensed enumeration.

13.8.title: The ज्ञानम् list begins — six neuter abstractions
13.8.body: अमानित्वम्, अदम्भित्वम्, अहिंसा, क्षान्तिः, आर्जवम्, आचार्योपासनम्, शौचम्, स्थैर्यम्, आत्मविनिग्रहः — neuter -त्व abstractions plus feminine -ता/-इ forms. The list runs without verb across 13.8–12, all governed by the eventual प्रोक्तम् at 13.12.

13.8.title: Negative-prefix virtues as ज्ञानम्
13.8.body: Three of the first qualities — अ-मानित्वम्, अ-दम्भित्वम्, अ-हिंसा — are formed by नञ्-तत्पुरुष, defining knowledge by what it *lacks*. Krishna's definition of jñāna here is strikingly ethical-behavioural, not epistemic — the *list of virtues* is itself the knowledge.

13.9.title: वैराग्यम् and अनहङ्कारः continued
13.9.body: इन्द्रियार्थेषु (सप्तमी बहुवचन) anchors वैराग्यम् — non-attachment *with respect to* sense-objects. अनहङ्कारः (नञ्-तत्पुरुष) adds the absence of ego-sense, continuing the privative pattern from 13.8.

13.9.title: Five-fold compound condensing the human predicament
13.9.body: जन्ममृत्युजराव्याधिदुःखदोषानुदर्शनम् is a single दीर्घ-समास: birth-death-old age-disease-pain-defect-perception. The constant *anudarśanam* (re-seeing) of these is itself ज्ञानम् — a Buddhist-resonant move within a Vedāntic frame.

13.10.title: Non-attachment qualified by family-specifics
13.10.body: असक्तिः and अनभिष्वङ्गः both denote non-clinging but the latter takes a सप्तमी locus: पुत्रदारगृहादिषु (in son-wife-house-etc.). Krishna names the precise objects where attachment hurts most, refusing to leave the virtue abstract.

13.10.title: समचित्तत्वम् — evenness across opposite events
13.10.body: नित्यम् (अव्यय, 'always') qualifies समचित्तत्वम्, with the सप्तमी बहुवचन इष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु ('in occurrences of the desired and undesired') giving the testing-ground. The द्वन्द्व इष्ट-अनिष्ट pairs the opposites the equanimity must cover.

13.11.title: मयि — locative of devotion
13.11.body: मयि (सप्तमी एकवचन) places the bhakti's locus on Krishna himself, qualified by अनन्ययोगेन (तृतीया, 'by undeviating union') and अव्यभिचारिणी (feminine -इन् participle, 'non-straying'). Bhakti is folded *into* the jñāna-list — a famously Gītā-specific move.

13.11.title: विविक्तदेशसेवित्वम् vs. अरतिः जनसंसदि
13.11.body: Two paired qualities: positive (सेवित्वम् = serving-ness, of secluded places) and negative (अरतिः = non-delight, in crowds, सप्तमी जनसंसदि). The contrastive structure makes the inner orientation visible — toward solitude, away from social bustle.

13.12.title: एतत् ज्ञानम् इति प्रोक्तम् — the list-sealer
13.12.body: The verse closes the 20-item list with another इति-quotative: एतत् ज्ञानम् इति प्रोक्तम् (कर्मणि कृदन्त). What was an unverbed catalogue across 13.8–12 is retroactively named ज्ञानम् — the iti-pattern lets a long list become a single technical term.

13.12.title: अज्ञानम् as the privation of the list
13.12.body: अज्ञानं यद् अतः अन्यथा — 'ignorance is whatever is otherwise than this'. The पञ्चमी एकवचन अतः ('than this') makes the definition purely negative: ajñāna has no positive content, only being-not-this-list. A clean philosophical move.

13.13.title: यत् ज्ञात्वा अमृतम् अश्नुते — the soteriological promise
13.13.body: The relative यत् … ज्ञात्वा (past absolutive of √ज्ञा) sets the condition, अश्नुते (लट्, आत्मनेपद, प्रथम एकवचन from √अश्) delivers the result: tasting अमृतम्. Knowing the jñeya is itself the immortality, not a path to it.

13.13.title: न सत् तत् न असत् — Brahman beyond the dichotomy
13.13.body: अनादिमत् परं ब्रह्म and the closing न सत् तत् न असत् उच्यते reproduce the Nāsadīya-style negative theology. Brahman is *not* called sat (since sat presupposes contrast with asat) and *not* called asat — both terms are tāntra-internal labels Brahman exceeds.

13.14.title: सर्वतः — quadruple omnipresence compound
13.14.body: Four सर्वतः-prefixed compounds (पाणिपादम्, अक्षिशिरोमुखम्, श्रुतिमत्, आवृत्य) saturate the verse with omnidirectionality. The structure echoes Śvetāśvatara 3.16 / Puruṣa-sūkta — hands, feet, eyes, heads, mouths, ears *everywhere*.

13.14.title: आवृत्य तिष्ठति — having enveloped, it stands
13.14.body: आवृत्य (absolutive of आ-√वृ, 'having covered') sets up तिष्ठति (लट्, प्रथम, एकवचन). The sequence — first envelop everything, *then* abide — captures Brahman's relation to the world: containment first, then transcendent stillness.

13.15.title: Six paradoxes in twelve words
13.15.body: Three contradictory pairs: सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासम् / सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम् ; असक्तम् / सर्वभृत् ; निर्गुणम् / गुणभोक्तृ. Each pair places Brahman simultaneously inside and outside an attribute — the language of *neti neti* worked through positively.

13.15.title: गुणभोक्तृ — agent-noun in -तृ
13.15.body: गुणभोक्तृ (कर्तृ-suffix, नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा) — 'guṇa-enjoyer'. The very same Brahman called निर्गुणम् two words earlier is called guṇa-enjoyer here. The grammar of -तृ agent-nouns lets Vedānta state the paradox without contradiction: free of attributes, yet experiencer of them.

13.16.title: बहिः-अन्तः, अचर-चर — locative-mode polarities
13.16.body: बहिः and अन्तः (अव्यय) place Brahman both outside and inside भूतानाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन). अचरम्-चरम् adds the unmoving/moving polarity. The verse builds Brahman by stacking the *both-and* of every spatial-kinematic opposite.

13.16.title: दूरस्थम् च अन्तिके च — far and near
13.16.body: सूक्ष्मत्वात् (पञ्चमी, causal: 'because of subtlety') explains why तत् अविज्ञेयम् — the subtlety *causes* the unknowability. Yet the same subtle thing is दूरस्थम् (far-standing) *and* अन्तिके (near, सप्तमी एकवचन of अन्तिक). Distance is collapsed by subtlety.

13.17.title: अविभक्तम् … विभक्तम् इव — appearance of division
13.17.body: The इव ('as if') is grammatically tiny but philosophically decisive: Brahman is undivided in beings, but *appears* divided. स्थितम् (कर्मणि कृदन्त, नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा) agrees with the implied तत्. The verse names māyā without using the word.

13.17.title: Three -तृ / -ष्णु agent forms for cosmic function
13.17.body: भूतभर्तृ (sustainer, -तृ), ग्रसिष्णु (devourer, -इष्णु), प्रभविष्णु (originator, -इष्णु) — three agentive suffixes naming the trimūrti functions in a single line. The same jñeya is creator, sustainer, and dissolver.

13.18.title: ज्योतिषाम् अपि तत् ज्योतिः — light of lights
13.18.body: ज्योतिषाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन of ज्योतिस्) — 'of lights themselves'. The Upaniṣadic motif (Muṇḍaka 2.2.10, Kaṭha 5.15) is quoted: Brahman is the light *behind* the sun, moon, fire. तमसः परम् (पञ्चमी, 'beyond darkness') seals the contrast.

13.18.title: Three terms collapsed: ज्ञानम् ज्ञेयम् ज्ञानगम्यम्
13.18.body: The verse identifies all three: it *is* knowledge, it *is* the to-be-known, it *is* what knowledge-reaches. हृदि सर्वस्य विष्ठितम् (सप्तमी एकवचन, 'set in the heart of all') localises this trans-categorial reality in the interior of every being.

13.19.title: इति … उक्तम् — the section seal
13.19.body: इति क्षेत्रं तथा ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं च उक्तं समासतः — the इति plus कर्मणि-कृदन्त उक्तम् (नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा) closes verses 6–18 as a unit. The three terms from 13.1's agenda are checked off: field done, knowledge done, knowable done.

13.19.title: मद्भावाय उपपद्यते — चतुर्थी of purpose
13.19.body: मद्भावाय (चतुर्थी एकवचन, 'for my-being-ness') is the dative of purpose; उपपद्यते (लट्, आत्मनेपद from उप-√पद्, 'attains, becomes fit for') is the verb already met in 2.3 in negative form. विज्ञाय (absolutive) sets the precondition: only after knowing this does the devotee become fit for *my mode of being*.

13.20.title: विद्धि repeated — double imperative
13.20.body: विद्धि appears twice (लोट्, मध्यम, एकवचन from √विद्), structuring the verse as two parallel commands: know prakṛti-puruṣa as both अनादी (द्विवचन, beginningless), and know विकारान् and गुणान् as प्रकृतिसम्भवान् (born from prakṛti).

13.20.title: उभौ अपि — both, emphatically
13.20.body: उभौ अपि (द्विवचन of उभ + अपि-emphatic) insists that prakṛti and puruṣa are *both* without beginning. This blocks any reading that derives one from the other — Krishna deliberately keeps Sāṅkhya's dualism intact before subsuming it in 13.23.

13.21.title: Two हेतु claims in parallel construction
13.21.body: Two parallel passive sentences: कार्यकरणकर्तृत्वे (सप्तमी एकवचन, 'in the agency-of-effect-and-instrument') प्रकृतिः हेतुः उच्यते ; पुरुषः सुखदुःखानां (षष्ठी) भोक्तृत्वे हेतुः उच्यते. Locative of *domain* + passive उच्यते = the technical 'X is *said to be* cause in domain Y'.

13.21.title: Division of labour: doing vs. experiencing
13.21.body: Prakṛti owns कर्तृत्वम् (agency); puruṣa owns भोक्तृत्वम् (experiencership). The -त्व abstractions cleanly partition the metaphysical work: matter does, consciousness undergoes. The kṣetrajña of 13.2 is now puruṣa, the kṣetra is now prakṛti.

13.22.title: प्रकृतिस्थः — situated in prakṛti
13.22.body: प्रकृतिस्थः (सप्तमी-तत्पुरुष, 'standing in prakṛti') explains *why* the experiencer suffers: it is located in matter. भुङ्क्ते (लट्, आत्मनेपद, प्रथम एकवचन from √भुज्) takes प्रकृतिजान् गुणान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन) — the puruṣa eats *prakṛti-born guṇas*.

13.22.title: गुणसङ्गः — the कारण of rebirth
13.22.body: गुणसङ्गः ('attachment-to-guṇas') is named as the कारणम् of birth in सद्-असद्-योनि-जन्मसु (सप्तमी बहुवचन, 'in births in good-and-bad wombs'). The षष्ठी अस्य pins it to *this* puruṣa. Attachment, not the guṇas themselves, drives saṃsāra.

13.23.title: Five agent-nouns naming the higher Puruṣa
13.23.body: उपद्रष्टा (witness), अनुमन्ता (consenter), भर्ता (sustainer), भोक्ता (enjoyer), महेश्वरः (great lord) — five -तृ agent-nouns climbing in cosmic rank. The same body's resident is reframed from passive भोक्ता (13.21) to महेश्वर.

13.23.title: परमात्मा इति च अपि उक्तः — the third name
13.23.body: Beyond the five functions, the verse adds two more namings via the iti-pattern: परमात्मा and पुरुषः परः. देहे अस्मिन् (सप्तमी, 'in this body') localises this Mahesvara *in* the body, repeating 13.3's identification: the knower in every field is the Lord.

13.24.title: एवं वेत्ति — the conditional knowing
13.24.body: य एवं वेत्ति पुरुषं प्रकृतिं च — the relative pronoun यः and वेत्ति (लट्, परस्मैपद, प्रथम एकवचन) take a triple direct object: puruṣa, prakṛti, and (with गुणैः सह — तृतीया + सह) the guṇas. Knowing must include all three layers.

13.24.title: सर्वथा वर्तमानः अपि — however he acts
13.24.body: सर्वथा वर्तमानः अपि (कर्तरि कृदन्त + अपि-concessive) — 'though acting in any way whatsoever'. न भूयः अभिजायते (लृट्-future-like in sense, here लट्) — 'is not born again'. Knowledge, not behaviour, breaks rebirth — a startling claim about adhikāra.

13.25.title: Three तृतीया methods for one आत्मा
13.25.body: ध्यानेन, साङ्ख्येन योगेन, कर्मयोगेन — three instrumentals naming three methods. Each takes its own subject (केचित्, अन्ये, अपरे = 'some, others, others'). पश्यन्ति (लट् बहुवचन from √दृश्) governs the first; उपासते (lat. आत्मनेपद) the second cluster.

13.25.title: आत्मनि आत्मानम् आत्मना — three cases of one word
13.25.body: Three forms of आत्मन् in one quarter: सप्तमी, द्वितीया, तृतीया. 'In the self, see the self, by the self' — the verse demonstrates that विभक्ति alone can carry an entire metaphysical claim. The case endings *are* the philosophy.

13.26.title: श्रुत्वा — absolutive opening the path of hearing
13.26.body: एवम् अजानन्तः (negative present participle, प्रथमा बहुवचन) — 'those not knowing thus' — are not abandoned. श्रुत्वा (absolutive of √श्रु) + अन्येभ्यः (पञ्चमी, 'from others') + उपासते names a *third* path: hearing from others and worshipping.

13.26.title: अतितरन्ति एव — even they cross over
13.26.body: अतितरन्ति (लट् बहुवचन from अति-√तृ, 'cross beyond') with एव-emphatic and the qualifier श्रुतिपरायणाः ('those for whom śruti is the highest resort'). मृत्युम् (द्वितीया) is the object of crossing. Krishna explicitly opens the door to those without independent realisation.

13.27.title: यावत् … तावत् implicit — the universal claim
13.27.body: यावत् सञ्जायते किञ्चित् सत्त्वम् — 'whatever existent comes into being'. The यावत् typically pairs with तावत्; here the तावत् is suppressed and replaced by तत् विद्धि. The structure makes the claim *universal in scope*: every born thing, no exceptions.

13.27.title: क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञसंयोगात् — पञ्चमी of origin
13.27.body: क्षेत्र-क्षेत्रज्ञ-संयोगात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन, 'from the union of field and field-knower') gives the *sole* origin formula for all स्थावरजङ्गमम् (immobile-mobile, द्वन्द्व). भरतर्षभ (सम्बोधन) re-anchors Arjuna for the metaphysical claim.

13.28.title: विनश्यत्सु अविनश्यन्तम् — two participles in case-clash
13.28.body: विनश्यत्सु (सप्तमी बहुवचन, locative absolute-style: 'amid the perishing') and अविनश्यन्तम् (द्वितीया एकवचन, agreeing with परमेश्वरम्). The participle pair stages the central seeing: seeing the imperishable *amidst* perishables.

13.28.title: यः पश्यति स पश्यति — the tautology that means everything
13.28.body: The relative-correlative यः पश्यति स पश्यति seems trivially tautological but functions as a strong claim: *only* the one who sees this way truly sees at all. Other seeing is not seeing. The grammar is rhetorical, not redundant.

13.29.title: न हिनस्ति आत्मना आत्मानम् — the non-violence of seeing
13.29.body: न हिनस्ति (लट्, परस्मैपद, प्रथम एकवचन from √हिंस्) takes आत्मानम् as द्वितीया object and आत्मना as तृतीया instrument. The seeing of the same Lord everywhere stops the self from injuring the self — a radical re-grounding of अहिंसा in metaphysics.

13.29.title: ततः याति परां गतिम् — पञ्चमी of consequence
13.29.body: ततः (अव्यय, पञ्चमी-like 'from that, therefore') links the cessation of self-violence to याति परां गतिम् (लट् + द्वितीया, 'goes to the supreme goal'). The supreme goal is *consequent* on the seeing-with-evenness, not a separate practice.

13.30.title: प्रकृत्या एव — instrumental of true agent
13.30.body: प्रकृत्या (तृतीया एकवचन) + एव-restrictive: 'by prakṛti *alone*'. क्रियमाणानि (कर्मणि कृदन्त, प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with कर्माणि) frames the actions as *being-done* — passive in form, prakṛti as agent. The verse reassigns कर्तृत्वम् from self to nature.

13.30.title: आत्मानम् अकर्तारम् — द्वितीया + द्वितीया apposition
13.30.body: Two द्वितीया एकवचन forms (आत्मानम्, अकर्तारम्) are apposed: 'sees the self *as* a non-doer'. The agent-noun अकर्तारम् in द्वितीया is itself the predicative complement — Sanskrit accomplishes 'X as Y' via case-agreement alone, no copula needed.

13.31.title: एकस्थम् — the locus of multiplicity
13.31.body: भूतपृथग्भावम् (the diverse-being-state of creatures, द्वितीया एकवचन) is the object of अनुपश्यति. एकस्थम् (द्वितीया, agreeing) = 'as standing in one'. To anu-paś (perceive *along with*, *in train*) is to track the many back to the One.

13.31.title: ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते — Brahman as goal
13.31.body: ततः एव विस्तारम् (पञ्चमी + द्वितीया, 'from that very [One], the expansion') + तदा ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते (लट्, आत्मनेपद from सम्-√पद्). Seeing the One *and* its expansion is the moment one *becomes* Brahman — सम्पद्यते is not 'attains' but 'becomes one with'.

13.32.title: Two पञ्चमी causal nouns
13.32.body: अनादित्वात् and निर्गुणत्वात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन of -त्व abstracts) — 'because of beginninglessness, because of attributelessness'. Two abstract nouns in the ablative explain *why* the परमात्मन् is अव्ययः and acts not.

13.32.title: शरीरस्थः अपि — concessive participle
13.32.body: शरीरस्थः अपि (कर्तरि-तत्पुरुष + अपि-concessive, 'though body-situated') sets up न करोति न लिप्यते. The double negation (does-not-act, is-not-stained) parallels the double cause from line 1. Bodily location does not impose bodily activity.

13.33.title: Ākāśa simile — सौक्ष्म्यात् as the operative cause
13.33.body: यथा सर्वगतं सौक्ष्म्यात् आकाशं न उपलिप्यते — पञ्चमी सौक्ष्म्यात् ('because of subtlety') is the precise mechanism. Space is everywhere yet untainted *because* it is subtle. The simile licenses 13.32's न लिप्यते by analogy.

13.33.title: यथा … तथा — the parallelism of non-staining
13.33.body: The यथा-तथा correlative pair maps space onto self: सर्वत्र अवस्थितः देहे (सप्तमी एकवचन of देह, 'in the body') तथा आत्मा न उपलिप्यते. The self abiding in the body is to body what ākāśa is to objects in space — present yet untouched.

13.34.title: Sun-simile — एकः रविः prakāśayati
13.34.body: यथा प्रकाशयति एकः कृत्स्नं लोकम् इमं रविः — the causative प्रकाशयति (णिच्, लट्) with एकः as restrictive: 'one sun lights *the whole* world'. कृत्स्नम् (द्वितीया एकवचन) emphasises totality.

13.34.title: क्षेत्री — possessive -इन् suffix
13.34.body: क्षेत्री (मतुप्/-इन्-derivative of क्षेत्र, प्रथमा एकवचन, 'field-possessor') is the kṣetrajña renamed for this simile. As one sun lights the whole world, one kṣetrī lights the entire kṣetra. The agent of consciousness is single; the field it illumines is all.

13.35.title: ज्ञानचक्षुषा — the instrumental that seals the chapter
13.35.body: ज्ञानचक्षुषा (तृतीया एकवचन, 'by the eye of knowledge') is the means by which अन्तरम् (the distinction, द्वितीया) between क्षेत्र and क्षेत्रज्ञ is seen. The instrumental ज्ञान-eye is what 13.1's questioner sought, now fully equipped.

13.35.title: भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षम् — द्वितीया of liberation
13.35.body: भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षम् (द्वितीया एकवचन, 'liberation from creatures' prakṛti') is the second object of विदुः (परोक्ष/लिट्-flavoured present from √विद्). ये … ते (relative-correlative बहुवचन) frames the chapter's final yājñikas: they go to परम् — the highest. Chapter sealed.
